lesson_title: Unsigned integers, and why to avoid them

cards.text: An {{c1::unsigned}} integer can only hold {{c2::non-negative}} whole numbers (0 and positive).
cards.source: 4.5 - Unsigned integers, and why to avoid them

cards.text: To define an unsigned integer, use the {{c1::unsigned}} keyword: unsigned int x;
cards.source: 4.5 - Unsigned integers, and why to avoid them

cards.front: What are the unsigned integer types in C++?
cards.source: 4.5 - Unsigned integers, and why to avoid them

cards.question: What does this code print (assuming 4-byte unsigned int)?
cards.explanation: Unsigned integers wrap around when going below 0. 0 - 1 wraps to the maximum unsigned value (2^32 - 1 for 4-byte unsigned int).
cards.source: 4.5 - Unsigned integers, and why to avoid them

cards.text: If an unsigned value goes out of range, it {{c1::wraps around}} using modulo arithmetic. This is {{c2::defined behavior}} (not undefined).
cards.source: 4.5 - Unsigned integers, and why to avoid them

cards.question: What's wrong with this countdown loop?
cards.answer: Infinite loop - unsigned i never becomes less than 0
cards.explanation: When i reaches 0 and decrements, it wraps around to the maximum unsigned value. The condition i >= 0 is always true for unsigned.
cards.source: 4.5 - Unsigned integers, and why to avoid them

cards.explanation:
Avoid unsigned integers because:
  • Wraparound behavior causes subtle bugs
  • Mixing signed and unsigned causes unexpected conversions
  • Doesn't prevent negative numbers (just wraps them)
  • Makes code harder to reason about
cards.source: 4.5 - Unsigned integers, and why to avoid them

cards.text: Best practice: Favor {{c1::signed}} integers over unsigned integers for holding quantities and mathematical operations.
cards.source: 4.5 - Unsigned integers, and why to avoid them

cards.front: When should you use unsigned integers?
cards.back:
Rare cases:
  • Bit manipulation
  • Well-defined wraparound behavior is needed
  • Interfacing with libraries that require unsigned
Generally prefer signed integers.
cards.source: 4.5 - Unsigned integers, and why to avoid them

cards.question: What does this print?
cards.explanation: When comparing signed and unsigned, the signed value is converted to unsigned. -1 becomes a very large unsigned number (wraparound), making it greater than 1.
cards.source: 4.5 - Unsigned integers, and why to avoid them

cards.back: Unsigned integral type used for sizes and lengths (e.g., sizeof, array indices). Despite being unsigned, prefer signed for counters.
cards.source: 4.5 - Unsigned integers, and why to avoid them
